full_response:
Let the laboratory (inertial) cylindrical coordinates be  

  (t , r , θ , z) with metric ds² = −c²dt² + dr² + r²dθ² + dz² .  

The rim of the merry-go-round has fixed radius r = R, so along the edge  

  ds² = −c²dt² + R²dθ².  (1)

1.  A local inertial frame comoving with a small element of the rim  
    Choose the tangential Cartesian coordinate  

  x ≡ Rθ , so that (1) becomes ds² = −c²dt² + dx².  

    The element of rim moves in the +x–direction with constant speed  

  v = ωR ,  β ≡ v / c ,  γ ≡ 1 /√(1 − β²).

    A standard Lorentz boost to the element’s instantaneous rest frame gives  

  ct′ = γ(ct − βx)  ⇒ dt′ = γ(dt − βdx / c)   (2)  
  x′  = γ(x − vt)   ⇒ dx′ = γ(dx − vdt )     (3)

    The spatial coordinate in that local frame is θ′ ≡ x′/R:

  dθ′ = dx′ / R = γ(dθ − ωdt ).                   (4)

2.  The relation between dθ′ and dt′ for a light ray  
    A light ray that skims the rim satisfies ds² = 0, so in the laboratory frame  

  dx = ±c dt  ⇔ dθ = ±(c / R) dt.               (5)

    Insert (5) in (2):

  dt′ = γ[dt ∓ β dt] = γdt(1 ∓ β).                (6)

    Insert (5) in (4):

  dθ′ = γ[ ±(c/R)dt − ωdt ] = γdt(c/R ∓ v/R)  
         = γdt c(1 ∓ β)/R .                        (7)

    Divide (7) by (6):

  dθ′ = (c / R) dt′  (in either direction).      (8)

    Thus, when light advances through an angle dθ′ in the co-moving frame, the
    proper time it takes is

  dt′ = (R / c) dθ′.                               (9)

3.  Time of flight for a full circuit  
    An observer stationed at θ′ = 0 emits two light pulses simultaneously
    (in his frame), one co-rotating (+) and one counter-rotating (−).
    In the laboratory frame the traversal times are  

  T₊ = 2πR / (c − v) ,  T₋ = 2πR / (c + v).      (10)

    The observer’s proper times are shorter by the usual time-dilation factor:  

  Δt′₊ = T₊ / γ ,  Δt′₋ = T₋ / γ.                (11)

4.  Radar circumference  
    The (one–way) radar length of the full circumference is defined as  

  C = c(Δt′₊ + Δt′₋)/2.                           (12)

    Substitute (10) and (11):

    Δt′₊ + Δt′₋ = (1/γ) [ 2πR/(c − v) + 2πR/(c + v) ]  
                  = (1/γ)  4πR c /(c² − v²)  
                  = (1/γ)  4πR /(c(1 − β²)).         (13)

    Using γ = 1/√(1 − β²) ,

    Δt′₊ + Δt′₋ = (1/γ) · 4πR /(c(1 − β²))
                = 4πRγ / c.                        (14)

    Insert (14) in (12):

    C = c · (4πRγ / c) / 2 = 2πRγ.                 (15)

Hence the observer on the rim assigns to the edge of the merry-go-round the
circumference

\boxed{\,C = 2\pi R \,\gamma = \dfrac{2\pi R}{\sqrt{1-\omega^{2}R^{2}/c^{2}}}\,}

exactly the same result that was obtained in lecture.

Confidence: 66%
